literal: to be done; that which is to be effected; the effect; from √kṛ + suffix -ya (gerundive)
invariant_definition: *Kārya* is the gerundive of √kṛ — literally 'that which is to be done' or 'that which is to be effected.' In technical Indian usage it has two principal senses, both indispensable. (1) In Pāṇinian *kāraka* analysis it is one designation for the *karman*-role: the effect or result the agent's action terminates upon. (2) In causal theory — and this is where the great Indian metaphysical disputes live — *kārya* is the effect-side of the *kāraṇa-kārya* relation: the produced or unfolded result whose ontological status is contested. The *satkārya-vāda* of Sāṅkhya holds that the *kārya* pre-exists in the *kāraṇa* and that production is merely manifestation (*āvirbhāva*) of what was already latent (Sāṅkhya-Kārikā 9: *asad-akaraṇād upādāna-grahaṇāt sarva-sambhavābhāvāt śaktasya śakya-karaṇāt kāraṇa-bhāvāc ca sat kāryam*). The *asat-kārya-vāda* (or *ārambha-vāda*) of Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika holds the contrary: *kārya* is genuinely new, a fresh *samavāya*-bound entity not present in its causes prior to production. Vedānta inherits the *satkārya* side of the dispute and then bifurcates internally over what kind of *satkārya*: *vivarta-vāda* (Advaita — the *kārya* is only an apparent transformation of *brahman*), *pariṇāma-vāda* (Bhāskara, Rāmānuja, Madhva, with variations — real transformation of cause into effect), and *avikṛta-pariṇāma-vāda* (Vallabha — real transformation without modification). The deeper conceptual point: *kārya* is never just 'effect' — it is the metaphysically loaded ontological status of the produced, which carries with it commitments about novelty, identity, and persistence.

primary_loci: On-disk Advaita primaries inspected, but no explicit kārya-locus was secured for this extraction

per_school.definition: Viśiṣṭādvaita treats the world's kāryatva as undeniable: earth and the rest are effects because they are composite, active, and marked by magnitude. [cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/14; cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/15] Precisely as kārya, the world and embodied agents ground the inference to one governing Īśvara, since the acit order follows one will and conscious agents act only under embodied conditions. [cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/16; cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/17; cite://yamuna/isvara-siddhi/18]

per_school.definition: Vallabha places nāma and rūpa on the effect-side: they are kārya-dharmas, while Brahman remains the samavāyi-kāraṇa throughout. [cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4] He then states that Brahman pariṇamates kāryākāreṇa without vikṛti, which is the school's avikṛta-pariṇāma account of the world as real effect. [cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26]

Sāṅkhya states satkārya-vāda explicitly: the kārya is sat because production does not bring an unreal thing into being, but manifests what is already grounded in its cause. [cite://isvara-krsna/sankhya-karika/9] The unmanifest is further established because the effect is constituted by the qualities of the cause, and because the system itself is articulated through the differentiation of kāraṇa and kārya within one evolving prakṛti. [cite://isvara-krsna/sankhya-karika/14-15]

Yoga inherits the Sāṅkhya understanding of causation: the guṇas are a kārya-kāraṇātmaka continuum, and kaivalya is described through their reabsorption once their work for puruṣa is complete. [cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.34] Accordingly, the effect-side is not eliminated as sheer non-being; it is resolved by pratiprasava when puruṣārtha is exhausted. [cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.34]

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treats kārya as the produced and non-eternal side of the causal relation, and locates cause and effect in samavāya rather than in a merely latent identity. [cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.7; cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.2.29] Vātsyāyana glosses the produced item as what comes to be after prior non-being, exemplified by ghaṭa and other effects, which is the classical anti-satkārya orientation of the tradition. [cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/5.1.37]

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, kārya is characteristically the śāstra-specified thing to be done: a ritual item or act has its own nirdiṣṭa kārya, so the term is primarily injunctive rather than a generic metaphysical label for produced objects. [cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/7.3.31] Śabara accordingly frames sacrificial discussion as asking what is to be effected by the yāga and teaches the production of apūrva through sacrifice, which is the school's operative sense of ritual kārya. [cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/7.4.1]

Mādhyamaka does not grant kārya independent svabhāva: to reify effect and cause is already to obstruct dependent origination. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.17; cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18] Kārya therefore survives only as a dependent designation within pratītyasamutpāda, not as a self-grounded product. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18; cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.19]

In the epistemological Yogācāra wing, Dharmakīrti ties what is accepted as svalakṣaṇa to causal efficacy: that which is designated as kārya-kāraṇa is the real particular, while exclusions are secondary conceptual constructions. [cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.172] Thus kārya marks efficacy at the level of the particular, not a persisting universal substrate. [cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.172]

Utpaladeva recasts kāryakāraṇatā in terms of manifestation: the inert has no power to bring the existent forth from the non-manifest, so causality is referred back to conscious agency. [cite://utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.4.2] The ĪPV gloss therefore identifies kāraṇatva with kartṛtva and kāryatva with karmatva, making cause and effect moments within Śiva's self-expression rather than externally related things. [cite://utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.4.2]

The school genuinely treats kārya, but no on-disk Aṣṭādhyāyī, Mahābhāṣya, or Vākyapadīya locus is available in this corpus

translator_note: 'Effect' is the standard gloss but disguises the ontological commitments. Saying 'effect' in English imports a Humean or post-Cartesian event-sequence picture; *kārya* is much more loaded — it presupposes a position on whether the produced is genuinely new (Vaiśeṣika) or pre-existed in latent form (Sāṅkhya/Vedānta). The other common error is conflating *kārya* with *karman*: *karman* is the patient-role within the act-grammar; *kārya* is the metaphysical effect-side of the cause-effect relation. They overlap (both are 'that which is to be done') but have different technical homes.
